aliases: 鸡蛋卷 / 薄饼 (Jī Dàn Juǎn)

chinese: 鸡蛋卷 / 薄饼 (Jī Dàn Juǎn)

food_subgroup: Crispy egg-coconut wafer roll; batter of eggs, sugar, coconut milk, rice flour, pandan; cooked on hinged iron mould and rolled while hot

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Kueh Kapit / Love Letters. Closest parent is thin wafer biscuit (FOODON:03301710 — wafer). No exact URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Biscuits and crackers — closest FoodEx2 parent for thin baked wafer snack; no specific code exists for Kueh Kapit.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Kueh Kapit. A03XK (Biscuits and crackers) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Kueh Kapit / Love Letters as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving is 3 wafer rolls (approximately 30g), providing approximately 126 kcal, 18.6g carbohydrate, and 4.8g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Love Letters / Kueh Kapit — Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Love Letters / Kueh Kapit. HPB Foodo is an approved primary regional source for Singaporean dishes under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice Flour-Based Products — analogous estimate based on rice flour wafer biscuit data
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kueh Kapit / Love Letters in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at ~55 (low) based on the rice flour base (GI ~72 for plain rice flour products) moderated by the fat content from coconut milk (11g saturated fat/100g) and egg protein, which slow gastric emptying and reduce the effective GI. Glycemic load per 30g serving = (55 × 18.6g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 10.2. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High saturated fat from coconut milk (11g/100g); low sodium (80mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content is low at 80mg/100g (24mg per 3-piece serving), posing minimal direct sodium risk, but the high saturated fat content from coconut milk (11g/100g) is a secondary cardiovascular concern for hypertension patients and should be limited to a single serving (3 pieces) per occasion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load per serving (GL ~10 for 3 pieces); high social over-eating risk
type2_diabetes.guidance: A single serving of 3 pieces (30g) provides 18.6g carbohydrate with a glycemic load of approximately 10, which is manageable for most people with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the light, dissolving texture creates a high risk of unconscious overconsumption during Chinese New Year social visits — 10 pieces (100g) delivers 62g carbohydrate and GL ~34, which is clinically significant and should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from coconut milk (11g/100g = 3.3g per 30g serving); cholesterol 120mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 30g serving contributes 3.3g saturated fat and 36mg cholesterol; while coconut milk medium-chain saturated fatty acids have a different metabolic profile than long-chain saturated fats, individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake to one serving (3 pieces) and avoid repeated servings during festive occasions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Love Letters, known in Malay as Kueh Kapit and in Chinese as 鸡蛋卷 (Jī Dàn Juǎn), are one of Singapore's most iconic and nostalgic Chinese New Year (CNY) snacks. They are gossamer-thin, shatter-crispy egg-coconut wafer rolls made from a batter of eggs, coconut milk, sugar, rice flour, and pandan flavouring. The batter is cooked on a hinged iron mould (similar to a waffle iron) over a gas flame, then rolled or folded into cigar or triangle shapes while still hot and pliable, and cooled to a perfect, delicate crunch. Originating from Peranakan (Straits Chinese) culinary tradition, Love Letters were traditionally handmade by families in the days before CNY and are now widely commercially produced and sold in airtight tins. Nutritionally, Love Letters are more energy-dense than their feathery weight suggests: at 420 kcal per 100g, the primary macronutrients are carbohydrate (62g/100g from rice flour and sugar) and fat (16g/100g, predominantly saturated fat at 11g/100g from coconut milk). A standard serving of 3 pieces (30g) provides approximately 126 kcal, 18.6g carbohydrate, and a glycemic load of ~10. The primary clinical concern is the social over-eating risk: each piece weighs only ~10g and dissolves instantly, making it easy to consume 10–15 pieces unconsciously during a CNY gathering, which would deliver 62–93g carbohydrate and GL 34–51 — clinically significant for people with Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. The food is gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo, the approved primary regional source for Singaporean dishes. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework with documented basis. Multilingual naming is complete for all five mandatory Singaporean languages.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Kueh Kapit / Love Letters is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026-04-13. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source. trans_fat_g is confirmed 0.0 per HPB source. vitamin_c_mg is confirmed 0.0 per HPB source — Love Letters contain no vitamin C-contributing ingredients. All core macros (protein_g 8.0, fat_g 16.0, carbohydrate_g 62.0) are non-zero and fully supported. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Kueh Kapit exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. Dietary tags include gluten_free (rice flour base) and dairy_free (coconut milk, no dairy). Halal status applies to standard commercial preparations without lard.
